text: The surgeon uses a robotic assistant to perform a sleeve gastrectomy, initially placing surgical trocars and retracting the liver to expose the upper stomach. The gallbladder and hiatus appear normal, with no hiatal hernia visible. The stomach is partially covered by the omentum. Robotic instruments, including a vessel sealing device, are introduced. The device cauterizes and cuts small blood vessels along the greater curve of the stomach, allowing access to the space behind the stomach. The surgeon divides blood vessels in the fatty tissue, changing direction to continue dissection.

The surgeon uses a robotic vessel sealer to divide blood vessels and mobilize the stomach along the greater curve, moving distally towards the pylorus. The stomach is then mobilized proximally, with several bites taken along the greater curve, while the fat pad is temporarily moved aside.

The robotic instruments are used to manipulate the spanning tissue, uncovering the upper part of the stomach. The surgeon continues to mobilize the greater curve of the stomach using the vessel sealer, advancing towards the fundus. The spleen comes into view as the surgeon carefully navigates the delicate area where the stomach is closely attached to the spleen. The robot's flexibility allows for precise maneuvering to expose the high posterior short gastric vessels, referred to as the pocket.

The surgeon uses the vessel sealer to delicately dissect the pocket area, carefully mobilizing the stomach away from the spleen without applying excessive traction. The diaphragm and hiatus are gradually exposed as the dissection progresses.

The surgeon completes dissection near the spleen, revealing the anterior border of the left cruce and clearing tissue around the hiatus. The stomach is repositioned for stapling, with the greater curve fully mobilized. The Ethicon surgical stapler, operated by Dr. Alvarez, is used to perform the first staple firing at the bottom end of the stomach. A 34 French bougie sizing device is advanced into the stomach to ensure a uniform sleeve as stapling progresses.

The surgeon advances the stapler along the stomach, aligning it closer to the bougie sizing device visible inside the stomach. The stapler fires, creating a uniform narrow sleeve while ensuring no constriction occurs. The surgeon inspects the staple line for any leftover staples and prepares the last bridge of stomach tissue for division. The stapler load is adjusted for thinner tissue near the diaphragm, completing the sleeve formation. The excess stomach tissue is set aside for removal, and preparations for a leak test begin.

The surgeon uses a device to occlude the stomach flow while the anesthesia doctor advances a nasogastric tube into the stomach. The stomach is inflated with methylene blue dye under substantial pressure, visibly expanding. The robotic arm assesses the pressure level, and the surgeon inspects the staple line for any blue dye leakage, indicating potential leaks. The sleeve shape is checked for uniformity and constriction. Minor oozing is observed at the first staple firing site, and controlled cautery is applied to stop the bleeding.

The surgeon uses the robotic needle dropper to place a figure-of-eight suture around a segment of fatty tissue containing large trunk blood vessels, aiming to prevent clot propagation into the main vein system. The vicryl suture is tied precisely to close off the vein and reduce potential bleeding. A silk suture is then used to tack divided tissue to the staple line, minimizing the risk of sleeve twisting post-surgery.

The surgeon removes the liver retractor and relocates the camera to a different position on the abdomen. The view shows the patient's left abdomen towards the right, with the mobilized stomach visible in the foreground. Using a large grasper, the surgeon spreads the muscle and lifts the staple line on the stomach, extracting the stomach through a small incision. The muscle is then closed with a suture using the Carter-Thompson Suture Passer, followed by infiltration of numbing medicine into the tissue.
